methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $46M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Housing & Construction (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 1 same-sector peers exist in Housing & Construction, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Inversion Space
private.rationale: Same sector (Housing & Construction), same stage (Series A)

private.name: Material Evolution
private.rationale: Same sector (Housing & Construction), same stage (Series A)

data.narrative:
Cobod, a Series A construction-technology company, presents a valuation challenge in a sector with limited comparable data. The Housing & Construction space includes only three private peers at similar or adjacent stages (ICON, Inversion Space, Material Evolution), with no public comparables available for direct benchmarking.

Given the scarcity of near-peer multiples, the analysis relies on stage-progression methodology. Using Cobod's $46M total capital raised as a valuation proxy and applying a typical Series A-to-next-stage step-up multiplier of 2.0x yields an implied valuation of $92M. This method is grounded in observable financing patterns rather than speculative revenue or margin multiples, which remain unavailable in the data set. The Peer-Median approach proved inapplicable; although three private peers exist in Housing & Construction, none meet minimum threshold criteria for same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-tier clustering necessary to produce a statistically defensible median.

The triangulation supports a valuation range of $78M (low) to $106M (high), centered on $92M. This range reflects uncertainty inherent to a single-method analysis in a nascent sector. Key sensitivities include the appropriateness of the 2.0x multiplier for Cobod's specific risk profile and capital efficiency relative to peers, and the extent to which ICON, Inversion Space, and Material Evolution represent true operational comparables. Additional revenue, unit economics, or market-share data would materially narrow this band.
